journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal of Nursing Studies Advances

title-group.article-title: The effect of digital health interventions in older adults with frailty: a systematic review and meta-analysis

contrib-group.aff: aSchool of Nursing, Gansu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Lanzhou 730000, China
bGansu Women and Children's Hospital (Gansu Provincial Central Hospital), Lanzhou 730050, China
cDepartment of Geriatrics, Shanghai University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Shenzhen Hospital, Shenzhen 518000, China

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author at: Department of Geriatrics, Shanghai University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Shenzhen Hospital, No. 16 Xiantong Road, Liantang Sub-district, Luohu District, Shenzhen, 518000, China. 65835066@qq.com

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Frailty is prevalent among the elderly population and can lead to multiple negative health consequences.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: This study compares the effects of digital interventions versus either non-digital interventions with the same content or routine treatment/care alone (or both) on improving physical function and frailty status in older adults, and clarifies the formal advantages and comprehensive benefits of digital interventions.

sec.title: Design
sec.p: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Systematic searches of the PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, CINAHL, PsycINFO, the Cochrane Library, and CNKI for publications archived in the database between its launch and January 16, 2025. The meta-analysis was performed using stata 15.0 and 16.0. We used the 2019 Revised version of the Cochrane risk-of-bias tool (RoB 2.0) to evaluate the study’s quality.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: 23 studies were conducted from 2007 to 2024 in 5 continents that investigated 3426 frail elderly participants were included in the review.The meta-analytic findings indicated superior efficacy in older adults with frailty for digital health interventions over control group on measures of the frailty scores (standardized mean difference (SMD)= -0.42, 95 % confidence intervals (CI) [-0.72, -0.12]; P = 0.006), grip strength (weighted mean difference (WMD)= 1.11, 95 % CI [0.09, 2.14]; P = 0.034), cognitive function (SMD= 0.12, 95 % CI [0.01, 0.24]; P = 0.038), and quality of life (SMD= 0.30, 95 % CI [0.11, 0.50]; P = 0.003). Subgroup analysis stratified by control group type revealed that the digital intervention combined with exercise group significantly improved frailty status (SMD= -0.52, 95 % CI[-0.96, -0.08]; P = 0.020), grip strength (WMD= 1.82, 95 % CI[0.53, 3.12]; P = 0.006), and cognitive function (SMD= 0.20, 95 % CI[0.04, 0.37]; P = 0.015) in elderly patients to a greater extent compared with the usual care group. There were no significant differences between digital health interventions and control groups on any of the remaining 5 comparisons: Timed Up and Go Test (WMD= -0.73, 95 % CI [-1.52, 0.06]; P = 0.07), gait speed (WMD= 0.02, 95 % CI [-0.05, 0.09]; P = 0.55), Short Physical Performance Battery (WMD = -0.17, 95 % CI [-1.14, 0.79]; P = 0.724), Sit-to-Stand Test (WMD = 0.19, 95 % CI [-0.69, 1.07]; P = 0.67), and depression (SMD = -0.21, 95 % CI [-0.47, 0.05]; P = 0.107). Meta-regression analyses revealed that covariates including sex distribution, country of origin, care setting, intervention type, intervention duration, and assessment methodology exhibited no significant moderating effects on grip strength, cognitive function, or quality of life outcomes.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: The study results showed that frail elderly individuals who received digital health interventions improved their frailty status, grip strength, cognitive function, and quality of life. Furthermore, compared with the usual care group, the digital exercise intervention group showed superior effects on improving grip strength, frailty status, and cognitive function among frail elderly populations, suggesting that the "digital intervention combined with exercise" model should be prioritized for managing geriatric frailty in clinical practice. Future research should conduct more large-sample, homogeneous, high-quality clinical trials to more accurately verify the effects of core digital modules on frailty and physical function in the elderly.

sec.title: Registration Number
sec.p: CRD42024614928 (PROSPERO).

list-item.p: Frailty is prevalent among the elderly population and can lead to multiple adverse health outcomes. It not only severely impacts their quality of life but also increases older adults’ healthcare demands.
Multicomponent exercise programs integrating aerobic training, resistance exercises, and balance drills are effective countermeasures against frailty.
Digital health technologies offer scalable, cost-effective solutions with particular relevance to resource-limited regions.
To our knowledge, this is the first meta-analysis to summarize the impact of digital health interventions on frailty in older adults.

The digital exercise intervention group showed superior effects on improving grip strength, frailty status, and cognitive function among frail elderly compared with the usual care group. This finding indicates that the "digital intervention combined with exercise" model should be adopted for managing geriatric frailty in clinical practice.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The global burden of frailty continues to escalate with population aging, significantly impacting clinical practice and public health systems (Hoogendijk et al., 2019). Frailty-associated healthcare costs exceed €12,000 per person annually (Mondor et al., 2019), with frail older adults demonstrating 4 additional general practitioner visits per year compared to non-frail counterparts (Roe et al., 2017). Substantial evidence links frailty to serious health risks like dementia, falls hospital readmissions, disability, and death (Denfeld et al., 2017; Ward et al., 2025; Warnock et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2025).

Frailty constitutes a multidimensional state marked by diminished physiological resilience and impaired ability to cope with stressors, reflecting cumulative declines across multiple organ systems (Warnock et al., 2024). Rodriguez et al. reported in The Lancet a prevalence of 7−16.3 % among adults ≥65 years, showing age-dependent progression and female predominance (Rodriguez-Mañas and Fried, 2015). Current diagnostic approaches primarily utilize the Fried Frailty Phenotype (FP) (Fried et al., 2001)and Frailty Index (FI) (Rockwood, 2005). Importantly, longitudinal data from the Irish Longitudinal Study on Aging demonstrates frequent bidirectional frailty transitions (Romero-Ortuno et al., 2021), with higher probabilities of reverting to pre-frail states than progressing to disability (Rodriguez-Mañas and Fried, 2015), highlighting the critical need for effective intervention strategies.

The integration of traditional healthcare with digital technologies has catalyzed digital health innovation. The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated adoption of contactless healthcare solutions, propelling digital health applications across medical domains (Shah et al., 2022). Digital Health Interventions (DHIs), defined as discrete digital functions targeting health objectives (Ambrosi et al., 2025), demonstrate considerable potential through their scalability and cost-effectiveness (Oakman et al., 2020). These encompass mobile applications, wearable devices, web-based platforms, remote monitoring systems, and activity trackers (Bai et al., 2024; Linn et al., 2021), enabling remote personalized care and improved healthcare access in rural/remote regions (Danbjørg et al., 2018; Russell, 2009). DHIs now facilitate assessment, health education, and symptom management for chronic conditions including cancer, diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, and mental disorders (Arshed et al., 2024; Guo et al., 2023; Hollis et al., 2017; Saevarsdottir and Gudmundsdottir, 2023).

Similarly, DHIs also play a crucial role in health monitoring and telemedicine for the elderly. For instance, mobile applications and activity trackers are used to monitor and guide the home-based rehabilitation of elderly cancer patients (Jonker et al., 2021), and online exercise programs are remotely provided for elderly individuals at risk of falls (Chan et al., 2023). Compared with traditional face-to-face questionnaire assessments and physical function tests, DHIs break geographical barriers through digital technology. They are not only more convenient to operate and more cost-effective, but also effectively reduce the burden of time and resources for patients seeking medical care, while alleviating the pressure of the shortage of medical personnel to a certain extent. Emerging evidence suggests Digital Health Interventions utility in automated frailty diagnosis and intervention (Kato et al., 2023). In detail, DHIs can develop personalized intervention plans based on the collected individual frailty data, and real-time track the effectiveness and dynamically adjust the strategies during the intervention process, which is of great significance for the long-term and continuous health management of the elderly. Multiple studies (Belleville et al., 2023; Kannan et al., 2024; Kerstiens et al., 2023; Piau et al., 2021) have employed DHIs for frailty assessment, monitoring, and management in older adults, though with inconsistent outcomes. Specifically, Piau et al. found no statistically significant differences in frailty parameters or quality of life between the intervention group (using smart insoles) and the control group. In contrast, the other three studies demonstrated that digital health interventions could effectively improve frailty in older adults. Current research primarily consists of small-scale controlled trials, with no existing meta-analyses synthesizing evidence on Digital Health Interventions' efficacy for multidimensional frailty. This study therefore evaluate the influence of digital health interventions on frailty progression in aging populations, aiming to inform evidence-based intervention strategies.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Prior to initiating this research, the study protocol received prospective registration in the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO: CRD42024614928). As all analyzed studies utilized data derived from previously published articles, the current meta-analysis did not require additional ethical approval or participant consent.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: We systematically searched seven major databases including PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, CINAHL, PsycINFO, the Cochrane Library, and CNKI, covering all available records up to January 16, 2025. Eligibility criteria included peer-reviewed full-text articles in English or Chinese, and excluding animal studies. The search strategy incorporated free-text terms, subject terms and controlled vocabulary using Boolean operator OR/AND. The following search terms were used: (elder* OR old*) AND (frail* OR frailty syndrome OR pre-frail*) AND (digital health OR digital health technolog* OR *health). Details are exemplified by PubMed in the Supplementary Materials.

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
sec.p: Inclusion criteria were: (a) The samples comprised older adults (age≥50 years); (b) Frailty should be ascertained according to internationally recognized diagnostic criteria (e.g., the Fried phenotype, the Frailty Index, or other frailty scales); (c) Study design types were randomized controlled trials; (d) The intervention group consists of any digital health intervention applied to older adults with frailty (remote monitoring, AI platforms, etc.); (e) The control group consists of non-digital interventions with the same content and/or only routine treatment/care; (f) The main outcome indicators were measurements of frailty and physical function.The assessment methods for frailty included the simple frailty questionnaire, Fried Frailty Index (FFI), Fried Frailty Phenotype (FFP), Study of Osteoporotic Fractures (SOF), Multidimensional Prognostic Index (MPI); The subcategories of physical function included gait speed, grip strength, the sit-to-stand test (SST), the TUGT, and the Short Physical Performance Battery (SPPB) score; (g) Secondary outcomes of interest were established cognitive function, quality of life and depression. The assessment methods for cognitive function included the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE), the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA), the Short Portable Mental Status Questionnaire (SPMSQ), and the Hong Kong List Learning Test-Total Learning/Delay Recall Trial; The assessment methods for depression included the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS-Depression) and the Geriatric Depression Scale - GDS-15 (GDS-15). The assessment methods for quality of life included the World Health Organisation Quality of Life (WHO-QoL), the health-related quality of life indexed by Danish tariffs (EQ-5D), the modified Katz Activities of Daily Living scales (Katz ADL), and the mental component summary (MCS-SF-12). (h) If the data of multiple studies were the same, the study with the largest sample size would be selected.
Exclusion criteria were: (a) literature in a language other than Chinese or English; (b) studies with incomplete data; or (c) the full text could not be obtained.

sec.title: Study selection and data extraction
sec.p: Two researchers (Liang and Cui) conducted independently initial screening, with discrepancies resolved by a third reviewer who evaluated the study and made the final decision. The data were collected from the included studies by two independent reviewers (Zhang and Cui) with a standardized data sheet including the following items: the author, publication year, Country, percentage of female, source, age, sample size, methods of intervention, duration, main outcome measures and secondary outcome measures.

sec.title: The risk of bias of the included studies
sec.p: Bias risk evaluation of the included randomized controlled trials was conducted by two reviewers (FX and ZJJ) independently applying the 2019 Cochrane RoB 2.0 guideline for bias evaluation in randomized trials, which included five domains of evaluation. They were consist of randomization process, deviations from intended interventions, missing outcome data, measurement of outcomes and selection of the reported results (Woldeamanuel and Oliveira, 2022). The risk of each domain was finally evaluated as low, high, and some concerns (Crocker et al., 2023). Conflicting opinions were settled by discussion involving an additional reviewer. The researchers will discuss whether to include studies with a high risk of bias.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: We recorded and analyzed the study data between December 2024 and March 2025 by using Stata 15.0 and 16.0. Continuous outcomes were analyzed using mean change differences (post-/pre-intervention) with standard deviations. Effect sizes were calculated as weighted mean differences for identical assessment scales or standardized mean differences for heterogeneous instruments, presented with 95 % confidence intervals (CI) (Murad et al., 2019). Dichotomous outcomes were synthesized using odds ratios or risk ratios. Between-study heterogeneity was evaluated through Cochran’ s Q test (significance threshold P < 0.10), with magnitude quantified by I²statistics: ≤50 % (low), 50−75 % (moderate), and ≥75 % (high) heterogeneity (Higgins, 2003). Fixed-effects models were applied when I²≤50 %, while random-effects models were used for higher heterogeneity (Yang et al., 2025). Results were visualized through forest plots.

sec.title: Publication bias
sec.p: Publication bias was assessed through funnel plot symmetry, complemented by Egger's and Begg's tests. Visual asymmetry and two-tailed P-values <0.05 indicated potential bias (Egger et al., 1997). When significant bias was detected or Egger's test proved inapplicable, the trim-and-fill method was employed to adjust effect estimates (Jennions and Møller, 2002).

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p:
Our study across seven databases identified 11,492 records. After deduplication, 9209 articles underwent title/abstract screening, with 64 proceeding to full-text review. Exclusions included 9 studies with incomplete data, 17 unavailable full texts, and 15 with outcome inconsistencies. Ultimately, 23 RCTs (Bailey et al., 2024; Bernabei et al., 2022; Gomes et al., 2018; Hagedorn and Holm, 2010; Kwan et al., 2020, 2021, 2024; Lin et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2021; Mollà-Casanova et al., 2023; Moreira et al., 2021; Osuka et al., 2023; Ozaki et al., 2017; Peterson et al., 2007; Pilotto et al., 2024; Rainero et al., 2021; Su et al., 2023; Tuan et al., 2024; Upatising et al., 2013; Valdés-Aragonés et al., 2024; Vestergaard et al., 2008; Yu et al., 2021; J. Zhang et al., 2022) met inclusion criteria for meta-analysis (Fig. 1).Fig. 1
Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta Analyses (PRISMA) statement of search.Fig 1

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p:
Twenty-three randomized controlled trials (n = 3426) met inclusion criteria for analysis. These studies, published between 2007 and 2024, enrolled participants aged 50–75 years. Geographic distribution included Asia (n = 11), Europe (n = 7), North America (n = 4), South America (n = 2), and Oceania (n = 1). Most participants were female (range: 48.3 %–100 %), with 19 studies having over 50 % female representation, including two all-female (Moreira et al., 2021; Vestergaard et al., 2008) and one all-male cohort (Peterson et al., 2007). Participants were recruited from communities (n = 3026), hospitals (n = 88), primary care clinics (n = 198), long-term care facilities (n = 16), day centers (n = 60), and senior associations (n = 38), with sample sizes ranging from 15 to 1519.Digital health interventions included exercise-based (n = 13), monitoring-focused (n = 6), cognitive training (n = 3) and combined exercise-cognitive programs (n = 1) (Rainero et al., 2021), delivered over 7 weeks (Gomes et al., 2018) to 3 years (Bernabei et al., 2022). Primary outcomes assessed frailty and physical function; secondary outcomes included cognition, depression, and quality of life (Table 1).Table 1
The characteristics of included studies.Table 1
Author(s), yearCountryGender [female(%)]SourcePatient ageSample size (n)Methods of interventionDurationMain outcome measuresSecondary outcome measures

SPPB: Short Physical Performance Bettery; TUGT: Timed up and go test; walking speed1: walking speed of 6 m;MCS-SF-12: mental component summary; SOF: Study of Osteoporotic Fractures;SPMSQ: Short Portable Mental Status Questionnaire; walking speed2: walking speed of 4 m; ADL:modified Katz Activities of Daily Living (Katz ADL) scales; MoCA:Montreal Cognitive Assessment; 5xSTS:The Five Times Sit to Stand Test; FFI:Fried Frailty Index;FFP:Fried Frailty Phenotype;GDS-15:Geriatric Depression Scale - GDS-15;walking speed3:walking speed of 10 m;EQ-5D: health-related quality of life indexed by Danish tariffs;HKLLT-TL/DRT = Hong Kong List Learning Test-Total Learning/Delay Recall Trial;MPI:Multidimensional Prognostic Index;HADS:Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale;WHO-QoL:World Health Organisation Quality of Life.

sec.title: Quality evaluation
sec.p:
In this assessment, 5 studies were judged as high risk, and 10 studies were assessed as having some concerns. In the randomization process, 1 study was deemed to have some concerns, while others were low risk. For deviations from intended interventions, 2 studies showed some concerns, with the remainder being low risk. In missing outcome data, 4 studies were rated high risk, and 4 others raised some concerns. For measurement of outcomes, 2 studies were high risk and 3 had some concerns. In selection of reported results, 5 studies exhibited some concerns, with others being low risk. The primary risk domain was missing outcome data, where 4 studies were classified as high risk due to exceed 20 % loss to follow-up or withdrawals (e.g., missing final evaluations from two hospitalized patients, leading to outcome dependency on true values). The risk-of-bias distribution is detailed in Fig. 2, Fig. 3.Fig. 2
Risk of bias summary.Fig 2Fig. 3
Risk of bias graph.Fig 3

sec.title: Frailty
sec.p:
Thirteen studies assessing frailty were included, comprising seven with continuous variables and six with categorical variables. Among the seven continuous studies, two utilized the Fried Frailty Phenotype (FFP), two employed the Frailty Index (FFI), one applied the Study of Osteoporotic Fractures (SOF) criteria, one used the Multidimensional Prognostic Index (MPI), and one adopted the Simple Frailty Questionnaire. Meta-analysis revealed that digital health interventions significantly improved frailty status compared to controls (random-effects model: standardized mean differences (SMD) = −0.42, 95 % confidence intervals (CI) [−0.72, −0.12]; P = 0.006; I²= 65.9 %; Fig. 4). Subgroup analysis stratified by control group type revealed that the digital intervention combined with exercise group significantly improved frailty status in elderly patients to a greater extent compared with the usual care group (random-effects model: SMD= −0.52, 95 % CI [−0.96, −0.08]; P = 0.020; I² = 74.8 %) (Fig. 27 in the Supplementary Materials 3). Sensitivity analysis excluding Justina et al. (Liu et al., 2021)confirmed robustness, maintaining significance under a fixed-effects model (SMD = −0.32, 95 %CI [−0.55, −0.09]; P < 0.0001; I²= 38 %).Fig. 4
Forest plot of Frailty (Continuous Variable).Fig 4

For categorical outcomes, five studies applied FFP and one used SOF. Pooled results showed no significant improvement in frailty status with digital interventions. (random-effects model: risk ratios (RR) = 0.94, 95 %CI [0.70, 1.25]; P = 0.671; I²= 51.9 %; Figure 5 in the Supplementary Materials). Subgroup analysis stratified by control group type showed no statistically significant differences in frailty improvement between the digital exercise intervention group and either the non-digital identical-content combined with usual care group or the usual care group (both P > 0.05). In contrast, the digital exercise intervention group demonstrated a statistically significant improvement in frailty compared with the non-digital identical-content group (random-effects model: RR = 0.70, 95 % CI [0.50, 0.99]; P = 0.041) (Fig. 28 in the Supplementary Materials 3).

sec.title: Timed up and go test
sec.p: Nine studies evaluating the Timed Up and Go Test (TUGT) were meta-analyzed. Pooled results demonstrated no significant difference between digital health interventions and controls in improving TUGT performance among older adults (random-effects model: weighted mean differences (WMD) = −0.73 s, 95 % confidence intervals (CI) [−1.52, 0.06]; P = 0.07; I²= 52 %; Figure 6 in the Supplementary Materials). Subgroup analysis stratified by control group type showed no statistically significant differences in TUGT improvement between the digital health intervention group and the usual care group, non-digital identical-intervention content group, or the mixed control group of the two (all P > 0.05) (Fig. 29 in the Supplementary Materials 3). Sensitivity analysis excluding Moreira et al. (2021)revealed significant improvement under a fixed-effects model (WMD = −0.76 s, 95 % CI [−1.28, −0.25]; P = 0.004; I²= 44 %).

sec.title: Short physical performance battery
sec.p: Six studies investigated the Short Physical Performance Battery (SPPB). Pooled analysis revealed no significant improvement in SPPB scores among older adults receiving digital health interventions versus controls (random-effects model: weighted mean differences (WMD) = −0.17 points, 95 % confidence intervals (CI) [−1.14, 0.79]; P = 0.724; I²= 85.2 %; Figure 7 in the Supplementary Materials). Sensitivity analysis identified substantial heterogeneity attributable to Bernabei et al. (2022). Exclusion of this study altered effect estimates but maintained non-significance under a fixed-effects model (WMD = −0.49 points, 95 % CI [−1.01, 0.02]; P = 0.062; I²= 0 %; Figure 8 in the Supplementary Materials). Further scrutiny of the study (Bernabei et al., 2022) demonstrated that its exceptionally large sample size (substantially exceeding other included studies), distinct population characteristics, and divergent data distribution patterns compromised the precision of pooled effect estimates, likely explaining the observed heterogeneity-driven bias. However, further subgroup analysis stratified by control group type on this basis revealed that the digital health intervention group significantly improved SPPB level in frail elderly patients compared with the usual care group (fixed-effects model, WMD= −0.84 points, 95 % CI [ −1.48, −0.20]; P = 0.01; I² = 0 %) (Fig. 30 in the Supplementary Materials 3).

sec.title: Grip strength
sec.p:
Nine studies nvestigating grip strength were meta-analyzed. Pooled results demonstrated that digital health interventions significantly improved grip strength in older adults with frailty compared to controls (fixed-effects model: weighted mean differences (WMD) = 1.11 kg, 95 % confidence intervals (CI) [0.09, 2.14]; P = 0.034; I² = 0 %; Fig. 5). Further subgroup analysis stratified by control group type revealed that the digital exercise intervention group significantly improved grip strength in frail elderly patients to a greater extent compared with the usual care group (fixed-effects model, WMD= 1.82 kg, 95 % CI [0.53, 3.12]; P = 0.006; I² = 0 %). In contrast, there were no statistically significant differences in grip strength improvement between the digital exercise intervention group and either the non-digital identical-content group or the non-digital identical-content combined with usual care group (Fig. 31 in the Supplementary Materials 3).Fig. 5
Forest plot of Grip Strength.Fig 9

sec.title: Sit-to-stand test
sec.p: Four studies valuating the Sit-to-Stand Test (SST) were meta-analyzed. Pooled results indicated no statistically significant difference between digital health interventions and controls in reducing SST completion time among older adults with frailty (fixed-effects model: weighted mean differences (WMD) = 0.19 s, 95 % confidence intervals (CI) [−0.69, 1.07]; P = 0.67; I²= 43.1 %; Figure 10 in the Supplementary Materials).

sec.title: Gait speed
sec.p: Seven studies investigated gait speed, and after the initial sensitivity analysis, six were ultimately included in the meta-analysis. Among these, two studies measured 6-meter gait speed, one assessed 10-meter gait speed, and three evaluated 4-meter gait speed. Pooled results demonstrated no significant improvement in gait speed among older adults with frailty receiving digital health interventions versus controls (random-effects model: weighted mean differences (WMD) =0.02 m/s, 95 % confidence intervals (CI) [−0.05, 0.09]; P = 0.55; I²= 52.8 %; Figure 11 in the Supplementary Materials). Subgroup analysis stratified by control group type showed no statistically significant differences in gait speed improvement between the digital exercise intervention group and either the non-digital identical-content combined with usual care group or the usual care group (both P > 0.05). In contrast, the digital exercise intervention group exhibited a statistically significant improvement in gait speed compared with the non-digital identical-content group (random-effects model, WMD= 0.10 m/s, 95 % CI [0.04, 0.16]; P = 0.002) (Fig. 32 in the Supplementary Materials 3).

Sensitivity analysis of the six studies revealed no single study significantly contributing to heterogeneity. Subsequent subgroup analyses stratified by sex composition, gait speed protocol, country, intervention duration, and setting were conducted to explore heterogeneity sources (Table 2). Subgroup analysis stratified by sex revealed that digital health interventions involving mixed-sex participants significantly improved gait speed in older adults with frailty (WMD = 0.06 m/s, P = 0.007; Figure 12 in the Supplementary Materials), whereas interventions with exclusively female participants showed no significant improvement (P = 0.188). Analysis by gait speed protocol demonstrated significant enhancements in 6-meter gait speed (P = 0.003; Figure 13 in the Supplementary Materials) but non-significant effects for 4-meter (P = 0.872) and 10-meter protocols (P = 0.707). Geographic subgroup analysis indicated that studies conducted in China reported statistically significant improvements in gait speed (P = 0.005; Figure 14 in the Supplementary Materials), while studies from other countries showed no significant effects (P = 0.993). Subgroup analyses of intervention duration and delivery setting identified neither as sources of heterogeneity (Figures 15–16 in the Supplementary Materials). Subsequent meta-regression analyses further confirmed no statistically significant associations for these variables.Table 2
Subgroup analyses by sex composition, gait speed types, country, duration of intervention and source.Table 2
SubgroupsNumber of studiesPooled Effect Size95 %CII2P value
Sex composition
all women2−0.06(−0.15, 0.03)4.2 %0.188
both sexes40.06(0.02, 0.10)24.3 %0.007*
Walking speed types
4-meter gait speed3−0.00(−0.06, 0.05)49.9 %0.872
6-meter gait speed20.09(0.03, 0.14)29.3 %0.003*
10-meter gait speed10.04(−0.17, 0.25)/0.707
Country
China30.08(0.02, 0.14)20 %0.005*
Other countries30.00(−0.05, 0.05)45.5 %0.993
Duration of intervention
≤3 months40.00(−0.12, 0.12)71.5 %0.974
>3and≤6 months10.04(−0.17, 0.25)/0.707
>6 months10.03(−0.03, 0.09)/0.345
Source
Community40.03(−0.05, 0.11)68.8 %0.521
Others2−0.02(−0.16, 0.13)0.0 %0.832

sec.title: Cognitive function
sec.p:
Nine studies evaluated cognitive function, with four using the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA), one Hong Kong List Learning Test-Total Learning/Delay Recall Trial, two Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE), and one Short Portable Mental Status Questionnaire (SPMSQ). Meta-analysis revealed a statistically significant improvement in cognitive function among older adults with frailty receiving digital health interventions compared to controls (fixed-effects model: standardized mean differences (SMD) = 0.12 points, 95 % confidence intervals (CI) [0.01, 0.24]; P = 0.038; I² = 0 %; Fig. 6). Further subgroup analysis stratified by control group type revealed that the digital intervention combined with the exercise group significantly improved cognitive function in frail elderly patients to a greater extent compared with the usual care group (fixed-effects model, SMD= 0.20 points, 95 % =CI[0.04, 0.37]; P = 0.015; I² = 0 %). In contrast, there were no statistically significant differences in cognitive function improvement between the digital intervention group and either the non-digital identical-content group or the non-digital identical-content combined with usual care group (all P > 0.05) (Fig. 33 in the Supplementary Materials 3).Fig. 6
Forest plot of Cognitive Function.Fig 17

sec.title: Quality of life
sec.p:
Six studies assessed quality of life using heterogeneous instruments: two employed the health-related quality of life indexed by Danish tariffs (EQ-5D), one World Health Organisation Quality of Life (WHO-QoL), one modified Katz Activities of Daily Living scales (Katz ADL), and two mental component summary (MCS-SF-12). Pooled results demonstrated a significant enhancement in quality of life for the digital exercise intervention group versus the usual care group (fixed-effects model: standardized mean differences (SMD) = 0.30, 95 % confidence intervals (CI) [0.11, 0.50]; P = 0.003; I² = 0 %; Fig. 7).Fig. 7
Forest plot of Quality of life.Fig 18

sec.title: Depression
sec.p: Two studies (Gomes et al., 2018; Rainero et al., 2021) evaluated depressive symptoms using the Geriatric Depression Scale - GDS-15 (GDS-15) and the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS-Depression), respectively. Pooled data analysis failed to show statistically significant difference of digital health interventions over controls in alleviating depressive symptoms among older adults with frailty (fixed-effects model: standardized mean differences (SMD) = −0.21, 95 % confidence intervals (CI) [−0.47, 0.05]; P = 0.107; I² = 43.6 %; Figure 19 in the Supplementary Materials).

sec.title: Meta-regression analyses
sec.p: Given the statistically significant meta-analysis results for grip strength, cognitive function, and quality of life, characterized by low heterogeneity and a sufficient number of included studies, we conducted meta-regression analyses for these three outcomes. Due to limited sample sizes, the Sidik-Jonkman random-effects model (Dent et al., 2023; Röver et al., 2015)was applied. Covariates hypothesized to influence effect estimates included: proportion of female participants, intervention pooled effect sizes for grip strength, cognitive function, or quality of life (Tables 3–5 in the Supplementary Materials).

sec.title: Publication bias
sec.p: Funnel plot symmetry and Egger's regression tests were performed for continuous frailty, gait speed, grip strength, Timed Up and Go Test, Sit-to-Stand Test, cognitive function, and quality of life. All funnel plots demonstrated visual symmetry, with Egger's test P-values of 0.660, 0.358, 0.143, 0.255, 0.294, 0.143, and 0.458, respectively, indicating no significant publication bias for these outcomes. For dichotomous frailty (categorical variable), Egger's test was statistically inapplicable. Trim-and-fill analysis using Stata 15.0 imputed two hypothetical "missing studies". Subsequent sensitivity meta-analysis showed no material change in the pooled effect (P = 0.262), confirming result robustness.

The Short Physical Performance Battery funnel plot exhibited asymmetry, with Egger's test revealing significant publication bias (P = 0.023). Trim-and-fill analysis detected no trim-eligible missing studies. Sensitivity analysis identified the study by Roberto et al. as an outlier (Bernabei et al., 2022). Its exclusion reduced heterogeneity from I² = 85.2 % to 0 %, with post-hoc bias tests showing non-significance (Begg's rank correlation: continuity-corrected Pr = 0.806; Egger's test: P = 0.868), confirming that SPPB bias originated from this single study rather than systematic bias.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The application of digital health technology in the intervention and management of frail patients has become a research hotspot in academic circles. As the first study to systematically synthesize clinical trial data on multi-dimensional frailty in the elderly receiving digital health interventions, this research conducted a meta-analysis by including 23 studies. The results showed that digital health interventions can significantly improve frailty status, grip strength, cognitive function, and quality of life in elderly patients, consistent with the research conclusions of Li et al. (2025)on cognitively frail older adults. To further explore a feasible, standardized clinical intervention plan, this study performed subgroup analysis stratified by control group type. The results indicated that the intervention model of "digital technology combined with exercise" demonstrated greater effects on frailty status, grip strength, and cognitive function in elderly patients, suggesting that this model is a preferred option for medical professionals in managing geriatric frailty. A categorical discussion on the 5-Time Sit-to-Stand Test (SST) (with only 4 studies included) also revealed that the digital exercise intervention group showed a consistent trend of numerically better improvement in SST across all studies, suggesting that it may have a potential positive effect on improving the physical fitness of the elderly. However, subgroup analyses on frailty (dichotomous variable) and gait speed indicated that there were no statistically significant differences between the digital exercise intervention group and either the usual care group or the non-digital identical-content combined with usual care group (both P > 0.05); statistically significant improvements in frailty and gait speed were only observed when the digital exercise intervention group was compared with the non-digital identical-content group. It should be noted, however, that the aforementioned statistically significant subgroups were each based on only one study, with a limited sample size and limited support. Consequently, the reliability and generalizability of the results are insufficient, and further verification through more high-quality studies is warranted.

Digital health technology can enable a range of interventions to support the management of frail elderly patients across clinical, community, and other settings. From the perspective of an ideal intervention plan, if medical conditions permit, digital health technology combined with multi-dimensional interventions such as physical function exercise, personalized nutritional guidance, and cognitive stimulation (Bernabei et al., 2022; Moreira et al., 2021) should be the preferred option. It can simultaneously act on the musculoskeletal, neurocognitive, and psychosocial domains of the elderly, which is highly consistent with the core dimensions of the frailty intervention framework (Dent et al., 2023). In this study, a digital health monitoring platform combined with multimodal interventions significantly improved frailty status in elderly patients (Pilotto et al., 2024). Meanwhile, it validated Li et al. (2025) on grip strength improvement in pre-frail older adults and corroborated Md Fadzil et al. (2022) regarding quality-of-life enhancements. Contrary to Jeong et al.'s network meta-analysis for cognition (SMD=1.21) (Jeong et al., 2025), our study observed a modest yet significant cognitive benefit (SMD = 0.12), potentially attributable to the integration of dual-task training (e.g., memory games paired with physical exercises) in our included interventions, whereas Jeong's interventions prioritized isolated cognitive training. Importantly, because there is no specific Minimum Clinically Important Difference (MCID) for our research context, this finding must be interpreted with caution. Our conclusion is therefore preliminary, and the observed effect may not yet translate to clinically meaningful improvement.

Starting from the practical needs of grassroots communities and hospitals, it is equally crucial to explore direct, effective models of frailty intervention. Among the 23 studies included in this research, the specific interventions combined with digital health technology can be categorized into four types: exercise intervention, single monitoring, dietary intervention, and exercise-cognitive training. Among these, the combination of digital means and exercise intervention plays a core role in the effective management of frail elderly populations. This supports the view of Lee et al. (2023) that "exercise is the key to improving frailty status and enhancing physical function." As a technical tool, digital health can promote behavioral changes by providing monitoring platforms, supervising the implementation of interventions, or enabling human-computer interaction.

Specifically, nine out of the studies included in this research showed that the exercise intervention driven by digital health was significantly meaningful for the improvement of physical function in elderly frail patients compared with routine care (non-exercise) (Bernabei et al., 2022; Gomes et al., 2018; Hagedorn and Holm, 2010; Kwan et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2022; Peterson et al., 2007; Tuan et al., 2024; Yu et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022). Two studies (Liu et al., 2021; Mollà-Casanova et al., 2023) demonstrated comparable outcomes between the digital health exercise intervention and the control group (including exercise training). However, Ozaki et al. indicated that among the frail or pre-frail elderly population, robot-assisted exercise was more effective than traditional exercise for enhancing both balance performance and lower limb muscle strength (Ozaki et al., 2017). This may be related to the higher precision and stronger professionalism of robot exercise. In contrast, the study by Moreira et al. showed that the routine group (exercise training) demonstrated superior efficacy in reducing frailty compared to the digital health exercise intervention (Moreira et al., 2021). This is because the exercise games used in this study focused on training cognitive function rather than physical function. Simple digital health monitoring did not improve the frail state of elderly patients compared with the control group (Upatising et al., 2013). Virtual reality cognitive training could significantly improve cognitive function and reduce physical frailty in the elderly compared with the routine care group (Kwan et al., 2021, 2024), consistent with the study by Jiajun et al. (Xue et al., 2025). Su et al. used a mobile dietary intervention for elderly frail patients, and the results showed that compliance with the Mediterranean diet improved significantly, and insulin resistance decreased slightly, but this change was not significant (Su et al., 2023). This result verified the finding in the study by Ni Lochlainn et al. (2021)that the Mediterranean diet plays a key role in the anabolic resistance of skeletal muscle proteins.

In-depth mechanistic studies have shown that physical exercise can alleviate age-related oxidative damage, reduce systemic inflammation, and promote mitochondrial biosynthesis (Angulo et al., 2020). Multi-component exercise programs encompassing aerobic, resistance, and balance training have emerged as effective interventions for improving frailty (Sadjapong et al., 2020; Taylor et al., 2023). A meta-analysis confirmed that the Otago Exercise Program (OEP) can significantly reduce frailty severity while improving balance, mobility, and grip strength (Yi et al., 2023). Although frailty can occur across all age groups, its prevalence is significantly higher in the elderly population (Hoogendijk et al., 2019), which provides a basis for prioritizing the elderly as the target group in intervention studies. Synthesized research findings confirm that exercise is a core intervention for reversing frailty in the elderly. Additionally, as an effective supplementary tool to usual care (Vo et al., 2019), digital health interventions also contribute to improving frailty in the elderly (Guasti et al., 2022; Md Fadzil et al., 2023). Compared with traditional exercise interventions, mobile health-based exercise programs not only overcome the spatial and temporal constraints of exercise for the elderly but also provide effective guidance and supervision, ensuring exercise safety while respecting patient autonomy (Ki and So, 2020). This is particularly important for pre-frail or frail individuals living in long-term home care or remote areas.

Overall, this systematic review has several strengths. Firstly, we conducted a comprehensive and systematic search of electronic databases, providing a more complete overview of existing clinical trial data. Secondly, we performed subgroup analyses stratified by control group type to identify specific effective intervention models, offering evidence-based support for clinical frailty management. Furthermore, we conducted sensitivity analyses and subgroup analyses for multiple included outcomes to explore potential sources of heterogeneity. However, our meta-analysis also has certain limitations. Firstly, the number of included studies is relatively small, precluding subgroup analyses by digital technology type (e.g., monitoring vs. remote intervention). The intervention effects of core digital functional modules on frail elderly populations remain unclear. Future network meta-analyses are needed for in-depth exploration to select optimal intervention models and enhance treatment precision. Secondly, our study exhibited heterogeneity, and the results should be interpreted with caution. This may be attributed to differences in geographical regions, study designs, and sample characteristics among the included studies. Additionally, all included studies focused solely on intervention effectiveness indicators and did not assess the role of digital interventions in long-term medical cost savings, thereby limiting the translational value of the research findings to some extent. Despite these limitations, this study still reveals innovative insights into the application of digital health technology in frailty intervention. Future studies should include more large-sample, homogeneous, high-quality clinical trials, further refine population inclusion criteria, and adopt a multidimensional outcome indicator system to more accurately assess the actual effects of core digital modules on frailty and physical function in the elderly.

sec.title: Personalized exercise for elderly with frailty
sec.p: While multicomponent physical exercise programs—encompassing resistance training, strength conditioning, aerobic exercise, flexibility training, and balance exercises—have been established as a core strategy for mitigating frailty in older adults (Guo et al., 2024), significant challenges persist in this field. Current clinical guidelines (Dent et al., 2019) recommend multicomponent regimens but fail to specify optimal dose parameters for individual exercise modalities, such as resistance training intensity, frequency, and duration. A study targeting frail older adults in China demonstrated a dose-dependent relationship between resistance training and improvements in muscle strength and physical health, with high-intensity resistance exercise showing superior efficacy in enhancing muscle strength (Lai et al., 2023). However, contrary evidence from Latham et al. revealed that high-intensity quadriceps resistance programs not only failed to improve physical function in frail older adults post-hospitalization but also increased the risk of musculoskeletal injuries (Latham et al., 2003).

Further, Binder et al. reported significant improvements in maximal voluntary thigh muscle strength and whole-body fat-free mass (FFM) among community-dwelling frail older adults following a 3-month progressive resistance training (PRT) intervention (Binder et al., 2005). However, such programs may be unsuitable for frail older adults with elevated total or visceral adipose tissue areas, highlighting the necessity of tailoring exercise prescriptions to individual physiological profiles. These findings underscore the critical need to balance safety, efficacy, and acceptability when designing exercise interventions. Consequently, developing optimized, personalized exercise prescriptions—integrating biomechanical profiling, metabolic adaptability, and risk stratification—represents a vital innovation for advancing frailty prevention and management in aging populations.

sec.title: Digital health enablement for frailty management in elderly
sec.p: Digital health interventions, as an innovative frontier in the era of digital medicine, have emerged as a cutting-edge approach for precision management of frailty in older adults. This field has established a comprehensive "screening-intervention-monitoring" framework through multidimensional technology integration. First, multimodal data-driven precision assessment enables early detection and dynamic prognostication of frailty. For instance, wearable devices and sensors capture digital gait biomarkers (stride length, cadence, etc.) to identify preclinical frailty states (Osuka et al., 2024; Yixiao et al., 2025). Second, intelligent algorithms enhance risk prevention. García et al. developed a CNN-LSTM neural network model that integrates video image features with inertial sensor data to achieve high-accuracy fall detection in older adults (García et al., 2022). Third, virtual cognitive-motor training systems, employing dual-task paradigms (e.g., exergaming), have demonstrated efficacy in improving physical function and mitigating frailty progression (Bailey et al., 2024; Kwan et al., 2021). The synergy of these technologies signifies a paradigm shift from experience-driven to data-intelligent frailty management.

Digital health interventions exhibit three core advantages in geriatric frailty care: (1) Spatiotemporal flexibility transcends traditional healthcare constraints by enabling remote frailty assessments and multidisciplinary care coordination via monitoring devices or mobile applications (Chang et al., 2021). However, implementation in underserved rural populations requires addressing literacy disparities and technological accessibility.To address this situation, we suggest optimizing from the grassroots level in the future: launching digital health pilots in villages/towns and communities. For example, we can set up guidance stations in communities to provide one-on-one training and information interpretation services; at the same time, we will develop adaptive tools (such as voice-interactive mini-programs and graphic guides) to help older adults build awareness and trust, thereby narrowing the digital divide.(2) User-centric design optimizes intervention adherence. Vikberg et al. demonstrated that personalized adaptive resistance training interfaces enhance engagement and satisfaction while preserving patient autonomy (Vikberg et al., 2022). (3) Cost-efficiency potential is evidenced by the STHER cohort study, where frail individuals incurred fivefold higher medical costs than non-frail peers over three months (Bock et al., 2016). Telemonitoring reduces unnecessary clinical visits, while AI-driven fall prediction systems lower hospitalization risks, collectively curbing healthcare expenditures. Nevertheless, device affordability remains a critical barrier for economically vulnerable older adults.

Collectively, digital health technologies offer scalable, cost-effective solutions with particular relevance to resource-limited regions. Their capacity to bridge healthcare inequities positions them as transformative tools for harmonizing global geriatric care standards.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first meta-analysis to systematically explore the impact of digital health interventions on frailty in elderly patients. The results of our study indicate that digital health interventions can improve the frailty status, grip strength, cognitive function, and quality of life of the elderly. Moreover, compared with the usual care group, the digital exercise intervention group demonstrated greater improvements in grip strength, frailty status, and cognitive function among frail elderly individuals, suggesting that the "digital intervention combined with exercise" model may be prioritized for managing geriatric frailty in clinical practice. Meta-regression analysis revealed that covariates such as gender distribution, country of study, care setting, intervention type, intervention duration, and assessment method did not significantly moderate primary outcome indicators, including grip strength, cognitive function, and quality of life. Future studies should conduct more rigorously designed longitudinal clinical trials to further explore the core digital modules that exert the most significant effects in this intervention model, thereby providing stronger, evidence-based support for the standardized, precise implementation of geriatric frailty management.

sec.title: Search formula of pubmed
sec.p: (((("Frailty"[Mesh]) OR (((((((((((((Frailties[Title/Abstract]) OR (Frailness[Title/Abstract])) OR (Frailty Syndrome[Title/Abstract])) OR (Debility[Title/Abstract])) OR (Debilities[Title/Abstract])) OR (pre-frail[Title/Abstract])) OR (Weakness[Title/Abstract])) OR (Fragile[Title/Abstract])) OR (Asthenia[Title/Abstract])) OR (Hyposthenia[Title/Abstract])) OR (fatigue[Title/Abstract])) OR (frail*[Title/Abstract])) OR (frailt*[Title/Abstract]))) AND (("Digital Health"[Mesh]) OR ((((((((((((((((((((((((((((((((((((((((Health, Digital[Title/Abstract]) OR (Digital Health Technology[Title/Abstract])) OR (Digital Health Technologies[Title/Abstract])) OR (Health Technologies, Digital[Title/Abstract])) OR (Health Technology, Digital[Title/Abstract])) OR (Intelligence Communication Technology[Title/Abstract])) OR (ICT[Title/Abstract])) OR (Artificial Intelligence[Title/Abstract])) OR (AI[Title/Abstract])) OR (mobile health[Title/Abstract])) OR (m-health[Title/Abstract])) OR (ihealth[Title/Abstract])) OR (eHealth[Title/Abstract])) OR (mHealth[Title/Abstract])) OR (digital*[Title/Abstract])) OR (tele*[Title/Abstract])) OR (Mobile applications[Title/Abstract])) OR (medicine APP[Title/Abstract])) OR (Online Wellness Program[Title/Abstract])) OR (Electronic[Title/Abstract])) OR (Electronic Program[Title/Abstract])) OR (wearable electronic devices[Title/Abstract])) OR (equipment[Title/Abstract] AND supplies[Title/Abstract])) OR (equipment[Title/Abstract])) OR (supplies[Title/Abstract])) OR (instrumentation[Title/Abstract])) OR (wearable electronic[Title/Abstract])) OR (electronic*[Title/Abstract])) OR (smartphone[Title/Abstract])) OR (smartphones[Title/Abstract])) OR (internet[Title/Abstract])) OR (internets[Title/Abstract])) OR (software[Title/Abstract])) OR (softwares[Title/Abstract])) OR (software design[Title/Abstract])) OR (web[Title/Abstract])) OR (compute*[Title/Abstract])) OR (Intervention[Title/Abstract])) OR (assist*[Title/Abstract])) OR (helping behavior[Title/Abstract])))) AND (("Aged"[Mesh]) OR ((((((((elderly[Title/Abstract]) OR (elder[Title/Abstract])) OR (older[Title/Abstract])) OR (senior*[Title/Abstract])) OR (geriatric[Title/Abstract])) OR (advanced[Title/Abstract])) OR (age[Title/Abstract])) OR (aging[Title/Abstract])))) AND ((((randomized controlled trial[Publication Type]) OR (randomized[Title/Abstract])) OR (placebo[Title/Abstract])) OR (RCT[Title/Abstract]))

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: None.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Meng-Yao Liang: Writing – original draft, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jin-Rui Cui: Software, Project administration, Methodology, Data curation. Xin Fan: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jun-Jie Zhang: Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology. Xiao-Ling Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration. Dong-Gui Liu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

Meng-yao Liang reports article publishing charges was provided by Shanghai University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Shenzhen Hospital. Dong-gui Liu reports a relationship with Shanghai University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Shenzhen Hospital that includes: employment. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: The data supporting this study's findings can be obtained from the corresponding author upon justified request.

fn.p: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ijnsa.2025.100470.
